coredata.dc:title: A peroxidase-like nanoenzyme based on strontium(II)-ion-exchanged Prussian blue analogue derivative SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanospheres and carbon quantum dots for the colorimetric detection of tigecycline in river water
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Ding
Fang, Xiaoyu
Li, Kang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, for the first time, we derived a composite of perovskite oxide SrCoO3 and Co3O4 by annealing the Prussian blue analogue exchanged with strontium ions and modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Its peroxidase-like catalytic activity was explored. The peroxidase-like activity was mainly evaluated by the rate of the chromogenic reaction. When H2O2 was present in the reaction system, the colorless substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was oxidized into a blue product (oxTMB), and this was monitored by UV–vis absorption spectrum. Among them, the SrCoO3 and CQDs not only promoted the generation of superoxide anion radicals (O2 −•) and electron-hole pairs (h+) in the reaction system, but also accelerated the electron transfer between the substrate TMB and H2O2. Therefore, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the reaction system was significantly improved. Moreover, the complexation of tigecycline (TGC) and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite enhanced the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the reaction system. Based on this, using TGC and TMB as template molecules, a molecularly imprinted colorimetric sensor for detecting TGC was constructed. The absorbance difference of the reaction system was linear with the TGC concentration in the range of 0.02–6.0 μM, and the detection limit was 4.46 nM. Furthermore, the proposed sensor had high selectivity and applied to the detection of TGC in Pearl River water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Strontium ion exchange
CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4
Peroxidase-like activity
Colorimetric sensor
Tigecycline

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Co–Co PBA and SrCoO3-500 (SCO-500)
Preparation of CQDs and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanoparticles
Study on the catalytic activity of peroxidase-like enzymes
Study on the steady-state kinetics of peroxidase-like enzymes
Reaction mechanism of the sensing system
Detection of tigecycline in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2019A1515012169 , 2021A1515011513 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81573678 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and HRTEM image (B) of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 NPs. SEM images of (C) MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and (D) NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4.
(A) XRD patterns of Co–Co PBA, Sr@Co–Co PBA, d-Co3O4, SrCoO3/Co3O4, and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4. XPS analysis of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4: (B) survey scan, (C) Co 2p, (D) Sr 3d, (E) O 1s, and (F) C 1s spectra.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of (1) TMB, (2) TMB + H2O2, (3) d-Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (4) SCO-500 + TMB + H2O2, (5) SCO-500/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (6) SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (7) CQDs + TMB + H2O2, (8) CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, and (9) CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (b) MIP/CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, (c) CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TGC + TMB + H2O2, (d) MIP/CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TGC + TMB + H2O2, (e) NIP/CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 + TMB + H2O2, and (f) NIP/CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4+TGC + TMB + H2O2. (C) The effect of different scavengers on the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4-TMB–H2O2–TGC reaction system. (D) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system solution, a–f: 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mg/mL. CV (E) and i-t (F) curves of (a) GCE, (b) SCO-500/GCE, (c) d-Co3O4/GCE, (d) SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE, and (e) CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE.

Steady-state kinetic analysis: (A) H2O2 concentration is 47 mM, TMB concentration variation range (0.015–0.428 mM); (B) TMB concentration is 0.15 mM, H2O2 concentration variation range (4.97–88.89 mM). The reaction rates of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 correspond to the double-reciprocal model of the concentration of (C) TMB and (D) H2O2. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system at different concentrations of TGC. a–j: 0, 0.02, 0.09, 0.3, 0.6, 0.9, 1.5, 3.0, 4.5, 6.0 μM. (F) The linear relationship between the difference in absorbance at 652 nm and the concentration of TGC, the illustration is the photo of the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system.
Schematic preparation process of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and colorimetric detection of TGC.

row.entry: C18 ACQUITY UPLC HSS T3 column

head.ce:title: A peroxidase-like nanoenzyme based on strontium(II)-ion-exchanged Prussian blue analogue derivative SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanospheres and carbon quantum dots for the colorimetric detection of tigecycline in river water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, 510006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, for the first time, we derived a composite of perovskite oxide SrCoO3 and Co3O4 by annealing the Prussian blue analogue exchanged with strontium ions and modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Its peroxidase-like catalytic activity was explored. The peroxidase-like activity was mainly evaluated by the rate of the chromogenic reaction. When H2O2 was present in the reaction system, the colorless substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was oxidized into a blue product (oxTMB), and this was monitored by UV–vis absorption spectrum. Among them, the SrCoO3 and CQDs not only promoted the generation of superoxide anion radicals (O2 −•) and electron-hole pairs (h+) in the reaction system, but also accelerated the electron transfer between the substrate TMB and H2O2. Therefore, the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the reaction system was significantly improved. Moreover, the complexation of tigecycline (TGC) and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite enhanced the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of the reaction system. Based on this, using TGC and TMB as template molecules, a molecularly imprinted colorimetric sensor for detecting TGC was constructed. The absorbance difference of the reaction system was linear with the TGC concentration in the range of 0.02–6.0 μM, and the detection limit was 4.46 nM. Furthermore, the proposed sensor had high selectivity and applied to the detection of TGC in Pearl River water.

ce:section.ce:para: Tigecycline (TGC), a derivative of minocycline, is the first new broad-spectrum antibacterial drug of the glycyltetracycline class, used in clinical practice against gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, anaerobic bacteria, etc [1]. At present, TGC is mainly used to treat complicated skin, soft tissue, abdominal cavity infections, and socially acquired pneumonia [2]. However, TGC is discharged into the environment through domestic sewage, pharmaceutical, and aquaculture wastewater, causing aquatic organisms to develop drug resistance, which seriously affects the ecological environment and human health [3]. Therefore, it is of great significance to monitor the TGC in the environment. Up to date, many analytical methods have been developed for the detection of TGC, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [7,8], fluorescence [9], colorimetric sensor [9], etc. HPLC is a common analysis method used to detect TGC, but it involves complicated pre-processing procedures and expensive equipment [9]. The colorimetric sensor has the advantages of convenient operation, high sensitivity, rapid response, and low cost. Ge et al. [9] developed a colorimetric sensor for detecting TGC based on the aggregation of gold nanoparticles induced by TGC, which has a wider linear range and lower detection limit, but the preparation cost of precious metal nanoparticles is very high. Therefore, we tried to prepare cheap and efficient mimic enzymes, combined with colorimetric sensing technology to achieve rapid, convenient, and highly selective detection of TGC.

Nanoenzyme is a kind of artificial mimic enzyme, which not only has the advantages of high stability, low cost, and easy preparation of nano materials, but also has the catalytic performance of natural enzymes on substrates [10,11]. Peroxidase-like enzymes are typical nanoenzymes. Colorimetric sensors based on peroxidase-like enzymes have been widely used in the fields of drug analysis [12], environmental monitoring [13], and biosensing [14,15]. At present, many materials have been proven to have peroxidase-like activity, such as precious metals, metal oxide (sulfide) compounds, carbon-based materials, and metal-organic frameworks [12]. Among them, transition metal oxides (such as Fe3O4, Co3O4, NiO, etc.) have the advantages of high stability, low cost, and controllable preparation, and are considered to be ideal mimic enzyme materials [16]. Prussian blue analogue (PBA, MII 3 [MIII(CN)6]2 .H2O, MII and MIII are transition metal elements) has the characteristics of diverse components, simple preparation, and large specific surface area. As a precursor, it has great potential in the preparation of functional metal oxide composite material. Through the ion exchange strategy, a small amount of metal ions can be utilized to substitute for part of the transition metal ions in the PBA framework structure, and the structure of the framework will not be changed [17,18]. Xue et al. [18] used Fe(II) ions to exchange Ni(II) ions in Ni3[Fe(CN)6]2 to obtain core-shell structure Fe4[Fe(CN)6]3@Ni3[Fe(CN)6]2 nanocubes. After high-temperature annealing, the Fe2O3-coated NiO/NiFe2O4 nanocubes were obtained, which exhibited excellent performance when used as a negative electrode material for lithium-ion batteries.

In recent years, perovskite oxides have attracted much attention as new functional materials. The molecular formula of perovskite oxide is ABO3, A is a rare-earth or alkaline-earth metal cation, and B is a transition metal cation. It has high ionic conductivity and redox activity, and has broad application prospects in the fields of solar cells, photoelectric catalysis, supercapacitors, and sensors [19]. Recently, many perovskite oxides have been proved to have excellent peroxidase-like activity and can be used in the field of analysis and detection [12,13,20]. SrCoO3 has excellent electrocatalytic properties [21,22], but there is no report about exploring its peroxidase-like activity. Therefore, it is necessary to explore perovskite oxide/transition metal oxide composite materials with excellent catalytic properties and use them to construct colorimetric sensors with high sensitivity. Based on the above strategy, we propose the following idea: introduce alkaline earth cation Sr(II) into the framework structure of Co3[Co(CN)6]2 (Co–Co PBA) through the ion exchange strategy, and a perovskite oxide/transition metal oxide composite material (SrCoO3/Co3O4) can be prepared through high-temperature annealing. In addition, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) had been proved to have peroxidase-like activity [23]. The surface of CQDs has abundant functional groups, which can be easily modified on the surface of functional materials to obtain mimic enzymes with excellent peroxidase-like activity. However, compared with natural enzymes, the biggest disadvantage of mimic enzymes is that they do not have sufficient substrate selectivity in the catalytic process, which also limits many practical applications of mimic enzymes. Molecular imprinting technology can prepare artificial receptors with the function of specifically recognizing target molecules, namely molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) [24]. Therefore, we can combine molecular imprinting technology to construct a colorimetric sensor that has a specific recognition capability for analytes and substrates.

In this work, we prepared a strontium ion-doped Co–Co PBA (Sr@Co–Co PBA) by ion exchange method, and SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite material was obtained after high-temperature annealing. After modifying CQDs, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) were obtained, and then the peroxidase-like activity was explored and the catalytic mechanism was discussed. We found that TGC could improve the catalytic activity of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 NPs on TMB. TGC and TMB were utilized as template molecules to generate MIP on the surface of the composite material, a molecularly imprinted sensor for detecting TGC was established. The experimental process is illustrated in Scheme 1 . As a result, the proposed MIP sensor had good sensitivity and selectivity, and had been successfully applied to the detection of TGC in Pearl River water samples.
Details about reagents and apparatus are described in the Electronic Supporting Material (ESM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Co–Co PBA and SrCoO3-500 (SCO-500)
ce:section.ce:para: Co–Co PBA was synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [17]. Details about the synthesis processes are described in ESM.

100 mg Co–Co PBA precursor was ultrasonically dispersed in 19 mL of double distilled water, and 1 mL 0.4 M SrCl2 solution was added. The mixture was stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The pink precipitate was obtained by centrifugation, washed three times with double distilled water, and dried at 60 °C to obtain Sr@Co–Co PBA.

Sr@Co–Co PBA precursor was placed in a muffle furnace and annealed at 500 °C for 3 h to obtain black particles of SrCoO3/Co3O4. In addition, d-Co3O4 was obtained by annealing Co–Co PBA under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: 32.4 mg 1,2-diaminobenzene was dissolved in 30 mL anhydrous ethanol/double distilled water (1:1) solution, and the resulting solution was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 180 °C for 6 h, the CQDs solution was obtained.

80 mg SrCoO3/Co3O4 was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL of double distilled water. After adding 5 mL of the above-prepared CQDs solution, sonicating for 30 min, and magnetically stirring for 2 h, the CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 nanoparticles was obtained.

15 mg CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, 0.028 mmol TMB, and 0.07 mmol TGC were added to 2 mL DMF and 1 mL methanol solution, and magnetically stirred for 10 min. Subsequently, 0.56 mmol 3-merraptnpropylt rimethnxysilane (3-MPTMS) was added to the solution, stirred for 30 min, and then 0.24 mmol tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was added and stirred for 20 min. Finally, 1 mL 0.1 M ammonia water was added to the above solution and stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. After centrifugation, the template molecules were washed away with acetone, washed twice with DMF and double distilled water, and dried to obtain MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4. Repeat the above steps without adding template molecules TGC and TMB to prepare non-molecularly imprinted polymer modified material (NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the catalytic activity of peroxidase-like enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the peroxidase-like activity of the prepared materials, 1 mg/mL suspensions of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, SrCoO3/Co3O4, and d-Co3O4 were prepared. 50 μL 1 mg/mL suspension of the above different materials and 100 μL 5 mM TMB were added to 3 mL 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer and mixed well. Then 200 μL 0.8 M H2O2 was added, the reaction system was placed in a 30 °C water bath for 10 min, and the absorption spectrum of the reaction system was measured with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. In addition, the effects of buffer pH, reaction temperature, and material synthesis conditions on the peroxidase-like catalytic activity of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite was explored.

Active species capture experiment: isopropyl alcohol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (PBQ), ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA•2Na), and L-Tryptophan (Trp) were used as trapping agents, which could effectively eliminate hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anion radicals (O2 −•), electron-hole pairs (h+), and singlet oxygen (1O2) in the reaction system. After adding the trapping agents to the reaction system, the absorption spectra were measured.

Fluorescence probe experiment: 50 μL MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite material at different concentrations, 200 μL 0.8 M H2O2, and 200 μL 5 mM TA were added to 3 mL 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer. After reacting for 10 min at room temperature, the mixed solution was transferred to a quartz cuvette for fluorescence detection. The experimental conditions were as follows: the excitation wavelength was 315 nm, the slit width of the excitation light and the emission light were both 10 nm, and the scanning range was 320 nm–550 nm.

Electrochemical test: in 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer containing 25 mM H2O2, the electrochemical behavior of different electrodes was recorded by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 50 mV S−1 in the voltage range of 0 to −1.0 V. By the current time method (i-t), 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer was utilized as the electrolyte solution. Under the applied voltage of −0.6 V, 100 μL 1 M H2O2 was added to the electrolyte solution every 20 s, and the electrochemical response was recorded. In addition, different modified electrodes were characterized by CV and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The scan rate of CV was 100 mV s−1 and the frequency range of EIS was from 1 to 105 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the steady-state kinetics of peroxidase-like enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: The absorbance of the reaction system at a wavelength of 652 nm was measured within 5 min by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer, and the absorbance was measured every 1 min. In the experiment, the concentration of TMB was maintained at 0.15 mM (or the concentration of H2O2 was maintained at 47 mM). Steady-state kinetics tests were performed in 3.3 mL 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer containing 50 μL 1 mg/mL CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and different concentrations of H2O2 (or TMB). The kinetic parameters of the catalytic reaction were calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation, 1/v = (Km/Vmax) × (1/[S])+1/Vmax, where Km is the Michaelis constant, Vmax is the maximum rate of conversion, and [S] is the concentration of the substrate. The value of Km is an indicator of the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate. The smaller the Km value, the better the affinity between enzyme and substrate.

50 μL 1 mg/mL MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 suspension, 100 μL 5 mM TMB, and different concentrations of TGC were added to 3 mL 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer and mixed well. Subsequently, 200 μL 0.8 M H2O2 was added to the solution and reacted in a water bath at 30 °C for 10 min. The absorption spectrum in the reaction system was measured with an UV–vis spectrophotometer.

50 μL 1 mg/mL MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 suspension and 100 μL 5 mM TMB were added to 3 mL 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer containing 4.5 μM TGC. Subsequently, 200 μL 0.8 M H2O2 was added to the solution and reacted in a water bath at 30 °C for 10 min, and the absorbance was measured by an UV–vis spectrophotometer. In addition, 4.5 μM TGC structural analogs (TC, MC, DOX, CTC, and OTC) and 45 μM potential interference (Lys, DA, Glu, BSA, and UA) were added to the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system to examine the selectivity of the sensing system.

The CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 were characterized by SEM and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR).

The morphology and microstructure of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, we can observe that many spherical nanoparticles are attached to each other. Among them, spherical nanoparticles with a particle size of about 20–50 nm and a porous structure is SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite, and nanoparticles with a particle size of about 10 nm are CQDs. The possible formation mechanism of SrCoO3/Co3O4 porous nanoparticles is explained as follows: the high-temperature pyrolysis process is mainly based on the equilibrium interdiffusion process [25]. A thin layer of SrCoO3 and Co3O4 composite formed on the surface of Sr@Co–Co PBA. The thin layer with pores allowed O2 to diffuse inward and the gaseous products CO2 and NxOy to be released outward, which promoted the conversion of the internal Sr@Co–Co PBA to oxidation products, thereby forming more pores. The HRTEM image is shown in Fig. 1B, the lattice fringes with spacing of 0.466, 0.286, and 0.243 nm correspond to the (111), (220), and (311) planes of Co3O4, respectively. The lattice spacing of 0.218 nm is attributed to the (100) crystal plane of CQDs [26], and the lattice spacing of 0.270 nm is consistent with the (110) plane of SrCoO3. This indicates that CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 NPs were successfully prepared. Moreover, EDX elemental mapping images (Fig. S1) of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite further confirm the synthesis of SrCoO3/Co3O4 and the loading of CQDs.

The surface morphologies of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 were characterized by SEM. As can be seen in Fig. 1C, MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 presents a very rough surface. This is because the imprinted cavities of TGC and TMB exist in MIP. However, the imprinted cavities did not formed in NIP. Therefore, we observe that NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 in Fig. 1D has a relatively smooth surface.

Fig. 2 A displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Co–Co PBA, Sr@Co–Co PBA, d-Co3O4, SrCoO3/Co3O4, and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4. All diffraction peaks of Co–Co PBA precursor are consistent with the literature [27], indicating that Co–Co PBA was successfully synthesized. The XRD pattern of Sr@Co–Co PBA is highly consistent with that of Co–Co PBA, which proves that the crystal structure of Co–Co PBA did not changed during the Sr2+ exchange process. In the XRD pattern of d-Co3O4, clear diffraction peaks can be observed at 2θ values of 19.1°, 31.4°, 36.9°, 38.6°, 44.9°, 55.7°, 59.5°, 65.3°, 77.4°, and 78.5°, correspond to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (533), and (622) crystal planes of the standard card Co3O4 (JCPDS no.43-1003), respectively [28]. At the same time, the diffraction peaks of Co3O4 can be observed in the XRD patterns of SrCoO3/Co3O4 and CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4. The diffraction peaks of SrCoO3/Co3O4 at 22.9°, 33.1°, 40.8°, 47.3°, and 58.9°, correspond to the (100), (110), (111), (200), and (211) planes of SrCoO3 (JCPDS no.38-1148), respectively. In the XRD pattern of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, we can also observe the diffraction peaks of SrCoO3. In addition, there is a broad diffraction peak near the 2θ value of 25°, which is the diffraction peak of CQDs [29]. The above results further indicate that CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite material was successfully synthesized.

The surface electronic state and the composition of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 are characterized by XPS. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 is mainly composed of Co, Sr, O, and C elements. The XPS spectrum of Co 2p (Fig. 2C) shows that the peaks of Co 2p3/2 are located at the binding energies of 779.3 eV, 781.0 eV, and 784.5 eV, and the peaks of Co 2p1/2 are located at 794.2 eV, 795.7, and 798.0 eV, and their satellite peaks are located at 789.0 eV and 803.7 eV, respectively. Among them, the peaks at 781.0 eV and 795.7 eV correspond to Co2+, and the peaks at 779.3 eV and 794.2 eV correspond to Co3+ [30], confirming the successful synthesis of Co3O4. The peaks at 784.5 eV and 798.0 eV are attributed to Co4+ [31,32], indicating the presence of SrCoO3 in the composite material. In the XPS spectrum of Sr 3d (Fig. 2D), the peaks of Sr 3d5/2 and Sr 3d3/2 are located at 132.0 eV and 135.5 eV, respectively [33]. The O 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2E can fit three types of oxygen. The peaks at 529.4 eV, 530.5 eV, and 531.8 eV are related to the metal-oxygen bond, the defect site of low oxygen coordination, and the adsorbed water, respectively [34,35]. As shown in Fig. 2F, the peaks at 284.4 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.9 eV are attributed to CC–C, C–O/C–N, and CO, indicating that CQDs were synthesized [23]. Detailed analysis of the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S2) is given in ESM.

In order to study the peroxidase-like activity of the mimetic enzymes, in the presence of H2O2, the oxidation performances of the mimetic enzymes on the substrate TMB were investigated. After the substrate TMB was oxidized to oxTMB, an absorption peak could be observed at 652 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Fig. 3 A shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized mimic enzymes. TMB (1) has no maximum absorption peak at 652 nm, and after adding H2O2 (2), a weak maximum absorption peak can be observed. The spectrum of d-Co3O4 (3) has an obvious absorption, indicating that it has peroxidase-like activity. We directly synthesized SrCoO3 (SCO-500) by high-temperature annealing method. Similarly, SCO-500 (4) has a weak peroxidase-like activity. Compared with SCO-500/Co3O4 (5) obtained by direct mixing, SrCoO3/Co3O4 (6) derived from Sr@Co–Co PBA has stronger oxidative activity for TMB. This is due to the full combination of SrCoO3 and Co3O4 and the large substrate contact area derived from MOF. Compared with pure CQDs (7), a significantly enhanced absorption at 652 nm is observed in CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 system (8), suggesting that CQDs can enhance the peroxidase-like activity of composite material. After characterization, the Zeta potential of CQDs in 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer is −22 mV, indicating that the surface of CQDs is negatively charged. While TMB contains two amino groups positively charged in the buffer, and has a strong affinity for mimetic enzymes with negative charges on the surface [36]. When there was no H2O2 in the reaction system, the mimic enzyme can oxidize TMB to oxTMB, which indicates that the mimic enzyme has oxidase activity [37]. It is worth noting that the CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4-TMB reaction system (9) can observe the absorption peak of oxTMB without adding H2O2, indicating that CQDs/SrCoO3/Co3O4 has oxidation activity. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the molecularly imprinted sensors are shown in Fig. 3B. After CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 (a) surface was modified with MIP and the template molecules were eluted, the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system (b) still has a high absorption intensity, indicating the formation of imprinted holes. When TGC is added to this two reaction systems, the curve (c) and (d) show significantly enhanced absorption intensity, which may be due to the coordination ability of TGC on the surfaces of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, which promotes the enrichment of TMB, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity of the reaction system [38]. Among them, MIP film can recognize TGC and TMB to resist interference from other substances. However, the surface of NIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 does not form the imprinted cavities of TGC and TMB, so no obvious maximum absorption peaks are observed in the reaction systems (e) and (f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reaction mechanism of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic mechanisms of peroxidase-like activity are mainly divided into two categories: the production of active species and the process of electron transfer. In order to study the catalytic mechanism, different scavengers were added to the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4-TGC-TMB-H2O2 reaction system to capture the active species that might be produced during the catalytic process. The intermediate active substances that may be produced during the catalytic reaction include •OH, O2 −•, h+, and 1O2. IPA, PBQ, EDTA•2Na, and Trp were used as scavengers for •OH, O2 −•, h+, and 1O2, respectively [16,39]. In the presence of the scavenger, if the corresponding active specie is produced during the catalysis process, the response signal in the UV–vis absorption spectrum will be greatly reduced. Fig. S3 shows the influence of the concentration of scavengers on the free radical trapping experiments. As shown in Fig. 3C and Fig. S3, after adding 25.0 mM IPA and 12.5 mM Trp to the reaction systems, the absorption intensities do not change significantly, indicating that almost no •OH and 1O2 formed during the catalytic process. However, the absorbance of the reaction systems decreased significantly after adding 25.0 mM PBQ and 7.5 mM EDTA•2Na, indicating that the active species produced by the catalytic reaction were O2 −• and h+. In order to further investigate whether the active substance •OH is produced during the catalytic reaction, fluorescent probe is a powerful tool for verifying •OH in the reaction system. Terephthalic acid (TA) was used as a fluorescent probe to capture •OH and generate highly fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which emitted unique fluorescence near 435 nm [40]. Fig. 3D shows the fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system solution. After TA was added to the reaction system, no absorption peak at 435 nm was observed in the fluorescence spectrum. And as the concentration of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 increases, the fluorescence intensity in the reaction system decreases slightly, which proves that •OH is not an active substance in the catalytic reaction. It is consistent with the result of the above scavenger experiment.

In addition, the catalytic abilities of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 and SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction systems were investigated under visible light and dark environment. As shown in Fig. S4A and B, under light and dark conditions, there is no significant difference in the absorption intensity of the SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system at 652 nm. For the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system, the absorption intensity of the dark environment is 89.3% of the light conditions. This is because the light-induced electrons on the surface of CQDs combine with the dissolved oxygen in the water to form superoxide radicals [41], which promotes the catalytic reaction.

In order to further explore whether the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs@ SrCoO3/Co3O4 comes from the electron transfer process, the electrochemical reduction behavior of H2O2 was studied by CV and i-t. As illustrated in Fig. 3E, SCO-500/GCE (b) and d-Co3O4/GCE (c) show higher current responses than bare GCE (a). And SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE (d) shows a significantly improved reduction peak current. When CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 (e) composite material was modified, the electrode has the highest current response. In the i-t curve (Fig. 3F), with the gradual addition of H2O2, the reduction current of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE rises most sharply. The above experimental results show that CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 composite accelerates the electron transfer between the electron acceptor (H2O2) and the electron donor (CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE). Therefore, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 accelerates electron transfer between TMB and H2O2. In addition, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, SrCoO3/Co3O4, and d-Co3O4 modified electrodes were electrochemically characterized by CV and EIS. Fig. S4C shows the CV curves of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE, SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE, and d-Co3O4/GCE in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl. The order of redox current Ip is: Ip(CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE) > Ip(SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE) > Ip(Co3O4/GCE). It proves that the modification of CQDs and SrCoO3 enhances the conductivity of the electrodes. The EIS results of different modified electrodes are shown in Fig. S4D. Compared with SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE and d-Co3O4/GCE, CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4/GCE has the shortest arc diameter, so its charge transfer resistance is the smallest, which is consistent with the CV results. The results of CV and EIS show that the modification of CQDs and SrCoO3 can effectively reduce the resistance of charge transfer and promote surface electron transfer, further confirming that CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 accelerates the electron transfer between TMB and H2O2.

To obtain the optimal experimental conditions for TGC detection, the following parameters were optimized: (a) pH value of buffer; (b) reaction temperature of the reaction system; (c) the concentration of Sr2+; (d) the amount of CQDs added during the synthesis process of composite material. In addition, the preparation conditions of molecularly imprinted polymer were also investigated: (e) the addition amount of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4; (f) the molar ratio of TGC to 3-MPTMS; (g) the molar ratio of TMB to 3-MPTMS; (h) the addition amount of TEOS. A detailed analysis of the optimizing experimental conditions is given in ESM. As shown in Fig. S5, the following experimental conditions were found to give best results: (a) the optimal pH value of HAc-NaAc buffer is 3.5; (b) the optimal reaction temperature of the reaction system is 30 °C; (c) the optimal concentration of Sr2+ is 0.4 M; (d) the optimal addition amount of CQDs is 5.0 mL. Similarly, according to Fig. S6, the best preparation conditions are: (e) the addition amount of CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 is 15 mg; (f) the molar ratio of TGC to 3-MPTMS is 1:8; (g) the molar ratio of TMB to 3-MPTMS is 1:20; (h) the addition amount of TEOS is 0.24 mmol.

The kinetic analysis of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 was performed by maintaining the concentration of one substrate H2O2 (or TMB) and appropriately adjusting the concentration of the other substrate TMB (or H2O2). As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the kinetic model is consistent with the Michaelis-Menten model of enzyme kinetics [16]. Through the Linewaver-Burk double reciprocal diagram (Fig. 4C and D) and Michaelis-Menten equation, the Km and Vmax of the simulated enzyme are calculated. As shown in Table S1, the Km value of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 for TMB is 0.176, which is lower than horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [42] and most nanoperoxidases, indicating that MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 has a high affinity for TMB.

Based on the peroxidase-like activity of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, when different concentrations of TGC were added to the system, it promoted the catalytic reaction of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4-TGC to TMB, and the absorbance value increased to varying degrees. In this way, a colorimetric sensor for detecting TGC was established. Fig. 4E shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the reaction system in different concentrations of TGC. As shown in Fig. 4F, the absorbance difference (△A) of the reaction system at 652 nm is linearly related to the TGC concentration. The illustration shows the photo of the MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system. As the concentration of TGC in the reaction system increases, the oxidation reaction of TMB is promoted, and more oxTMB is produced. The color of the system gradually deepen, and the absorbance of the absorption spectrum at 652 nm also increases. In the TGC concentration range of 0.02–6.0 μM, the linear equation is △A = 0.0988C + 0.0316 (μM), and the detection limit (LOD) is 4.46 nM (LOD = 3σ/S), where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 10), and S is the slope of the calibration curve. Table 1 compares the linear range and LOD of this method with other reported methods for TGC detection [4–9]. The proposed MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 colorimetric sensor has a wider linear range and a lower detection limit. Compared with liquid chromatography and fluorescence sensor, MIP/CQDs@ SrCoO3/Co3O4 colorimetric sensor has the lowest detection limits. And compared with the colorimetric sensor constructed by the precious metal Au NPs, the MIP colorimetric sensor has a lower manufacturing cost.

In order to study the selectivity of the sensor, the effects of structural analogs of TGC and potential interfering substances (TC, MC, DOX, CTC, OTC, Lys, DA, Glu, BSA, and UA) on the detection results were further explored. As shown in Fig. S7, the additions of 4.5 μM TGC structural analogues and 45 μM potential interfering substances do not affect the determination of TGC. It shows that MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system has high selectivity to TGC in the presence of other interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of tigecycline in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the feasibility of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 reaction system in actual sample detection, this colorimetric sensor was applied to detect TCG in river water samples (from Pearl River water). The river water samples were diluted 25 times with 0.2 M HAc-NaAc (pH 3.5) buffer before the determination. As shown in Table S2, after adding 0.3, 1.0, and 3.5 μM TCG to 10 mL diluted river water samples, the recoveries are 98.0–102.7%. The RSDs are lower than 5%, indicating that the method can be applied to detect TGC in complex environmental sample.

Herein, we had developed an effective strategy for the synthesis of perovskite oxide composite (SrCoO3/Co3O4) based on ion exchange PBA (Sr@Co–Co PBA). After it was modified with CQDs and MIP, based on the excellent peroxidase-like activity of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4, a colorimetric sensor with high selectivity and high sensitivity for detecting TGC was constructed. The sensor has a wide linear range, low detection limit, and good anti-interference ability, and had been successfully applied to the detection of TGC in Pearl River water samples, further proving its great potential in environmental monitoring. However, the preparation process of MIP/CQDs@SrCoO3/Co3O4 is slightly cumbersome. Therefore, we will try to develop a new effective strategy with fewer synthesis steps, or explore other types of perovskite oxide composite materials to prepare catalytic materials with excellent performance.
Ding Duan: Experiments, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiaoyu Fang: Validation, Investigation. Kang Li: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2019A1515012169, 2021A1515011513) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81573678).

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bifunctionalized mesoporous silica-supported gold nanoparticles: intrinsic oxidase and peroxidase catalytic activities for antibacterial applications[J]
